name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ وَسَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰ عِبَادِهِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱصْطَفَىٰٓ ۗ ءَآللَّهُ خَيْرٌ أَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٥٩)
```

```english
Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, and Peace be to the servants whom He has chosen.’ Is Allah better, or the partners they ascribe [to Him]? (59)
```

```arabic
أَمَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَأَنزَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَنۢبَتْنَا بِهِۦ حَدَآئِقَ ذَاتَ بَهْجَةٍ مَّا كَانَ لَكُمْ أَن تُنۢبِتُوا۟ شَجَرَهَآ ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ يَعْدِلُونَ (٦٠)
```

```english
Is He who created the heavens and the earth, and sends down for you water from the sky, whereby We grow delightful gardens, whose trees you could never cause to grow...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Indeed, they are a lot who equate [others with Allah]. (60)
```

```arabic
أَمَّن جَعَلَ ٱلْأَرْضَ قَرَارًا وَجَعَلَ خِلَـٰلَهَآ أَنْهَـٰرًا وَجَعَلَ لَهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ حَاجِزًا ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٦١)
```

```english
Is He who made the earth an abode [for you], and made rivers [flowing] through it, and set firm mountains for it [’s stability], and set a barrier between the two seas...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Indeed, most of them do not know. (61)
```

```arabic
أَمَّن يُجِيبُ ٱلْمُضْطَرَّ إِذَا دَعَاهُ وَيَكْشِفُ ٱلسُّوٓءَ وَيَجْعَلُكُمْ خُلَفَآءَ ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٦٢)
```

```english
Is He who answers the call of the distressed [person] when he invokes Him and removes his distress, and makes you successors on the earth...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Little is the admonition that you take. (62)
```

```arabic
أَمَّن يَهْدِيكُمْ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ وَمَن يُرْسِلُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ بُشْرًۢا بَيْنَ يَدَىْ رَحْمَتِهِۦٓ ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ تَعَـٰلَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٦٣)
```

```english
Is He who guides you in the darkness of land and sea and who sends the winds as harbingers of His mercy...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Exalted is Allah above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (63)
```

```arabic
أَمَّن يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ وَمَن يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قُلْ هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٦٤)
```

```english
Is He who originates the creation, then He will bring it back, and who provides for you from the sky and the earth...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Say, ‘Produce your evidence, if you are truthful.’ (64)
```

```arabic
قُل لَّا يَعْلَمُ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلْغَيْبَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ (٦٥)
```

```english
Say, ‘No one in the heavens or the earth knows the Unseen except Allah, and they are not aware when they will be resurrected.’ (65)
```

```arabic
بَلِ ٱدَّٰرَكَ عِلْمُهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ فِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهَا ۖ بَلْ هُم مِّنْهَا عَمُونَ (٦٦)
```

```english
Do they comprehend the knowledge of the Hereafter? No, they are in doubt about it. Indeed, they are blind to it. (66)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَءِذَا كُنَّا تُرَٰبًا وَءَابَآؤُنَآ أَئِنَّا لَمُخْرَجُونَ (٦٧)
```

```english
The faithless say, ‘What! When we and our fathers have become dust shall we be raised [from the dead]? (67)
```

```arabic
لَقَدْ وُعِدْنَا هَـٰذَا نَحْنُ وَءَابَآؤُنَا مِن قَبْلُ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٦٨)
```

```english
We and our fathers were promised this before. [But] these are just myths of the ancients.’ (68)
```

```arabic
قُلْ سِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٦٩)
```

```english
Say, ‘Travel over the land and observe how was the fate of the guilty.’ (69)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا تَكُن فِى ضَيْقٍ مِّمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ (٧٠)
```

```english
Do not grieve for them, and do not be upset by their guile. (70)
```

```arabic
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٧١)
```

```english
They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ (71)
```

```arabic
قُلْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ رَدِفَ لَكُم بَعْضُ ٱلَّذِى تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ (٧٢)
```

```english
Say, ‘Perhaps there is right behind you some of what you seek to hasten.’ (72)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٧٣)
```

```english
Indeed your Lord is gracious to mankind, but most of them do not give thanks. (73)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَيَعْلَمُ مَا تُكِنُّ صُدُورُهُمْ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ (٧٤)
```

```english
Your Lord knows whatever their breasts conceal, and whatever they disclose. (74)
```

```arabic
وَمَا مِنْ غَآئِبَةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ (٧٥)
```

```english
There is no invisible thing in the heaven and the earth but it is in a manifest Book. (75)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ يَقُصُّ عَلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَكْثَرَ ٱلَّذِى هُمْ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (٧٦)
```

```english
This Quran recounts for the Children of Israel most of what they differ about, (76)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٧٧)
```

```english
and it is indeed a guidance and mercy for the faithful. (77)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِى بَيْنَهُم بِحُكْمِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٧٨)
```

```english
Your Lord will decide between them by His judgement, and He is the All-mighty, the All-knowing. (78)
```

```arabic
فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَى ٱلْحَقِّ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٧٩)
```

```english
So put your trust in Allah, for you indeed stand on the manifest truth. (79)
```

```arabic
إِنَّكَ لَا تُسْمِعُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَلَا تُسْمِعُ ٱلصُّمَّ ٱلدُّعَآءَ إِذَا وَلَّوْا۟ مُدْبِرِينَ (٨٠)
```

```english
You cannot make the dead hear, nor can you make the deaf listen to your call when they turn their backs, (80)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَنتَ بِهَـٰدِى ٱلْعُمْىِ عَن ضَلَـٰلَتِهِمْ ۖ إِن تُسْمِعُ إِلَّا مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَهُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (٨١)
```

```english
nor can you lead the blind out of their error. You can make only those hear you who believe in Our signs and have submitted. (81)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a transition from the stories He, the Glorified, has narrated—which are exemplars of His established sunnah (السنة) — *the habitual way of Allah’s action* — prevailing in the human species regarding His guiding them and showing them the path of their felicity in life, His honoring those among them who were guided to the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — through election (الاصطفاء) and magnificent favors, and His seizing those who associated partners with Him, turned away from His remembrance, and plotted against Him with the punishment of extirpation and painful exemplary chastisement.

The transition moves toward His praise and peace upon His elect servants, and the affirmation that He alone is worthy of servitude (العبودية) — *the state of total submission and devotion to Allah* — to the exclusion of others whom they associate with Him. Then, the discourse continues regarding monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — the proof of the Return (المعاد) — *the restoration of life for judgment* — and related matters of diverse true cognitions (المعارف). Thus, the context of the verses of this Surah is similar to the context found in Surah Maryam, as previously discussed.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ وَسَلامٌ عَلى عِبادِهِ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفى آللهُ خَيْرٌ أَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** — *Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, and peace be to His servants whom He has chosen.’ Is Allah better, or the partners they ascribe [to Him]?* (27:59). When He narrated the stories of the prophets and their nations—containing the explanation of His established sunnah prevailing in past nations, what He did for the believers among them in terms of election and increased favor (as with the prophets among them), and what He did to the disbelievers in terms of punishment and destruction—and since He has done nothing but beautiful good, nor has His sunnah run except according to profound wisdom (الحكمة البالغة), He transitioned from there to commanding His Prophet to praise Him, to laud Him, and to send peace upon the elect among His servants, affirming that He, the Exalted, is the one designated for worship (العبادة).

It is a transition from stories to praise, salutation, and monotheism; it is not a logical deduction (استنتاج), even if it functions as one. Otherwise, it would have been said: "So say, 'All praise belongs to Allah...'" or "So is Allah better...".

His saying: **قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ** (Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah’) is a command to praise Him, and it involves returning every praise to Him, the Exalted. This is because the preceding verses established that the ultimate return of all creation (خلق) and governance (تدبير) is to Him; He is the Bestower of every good by His wisdom and the Doer of every beautiful act by His power.

His saying: **وَسَلامٌ عَلى عِبادِهِ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفى** (and peace be to His servants whom He has chosen) is conjoined to what precedes it as part of the commanded speech. In the salutation (التسليم) upon those elect servants, there is a negation of all aspects of resistance and opposition within the soul of the one who offers peace toward the divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية) they possess and its beautiful effects—as necessitated by the meaning of peace (السلام). Thus, in the command to offer peace, there is an implicit command to prepare oneself to accept the guidance and its effects that they possess. In a sense, it carries the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَدَى اللهُ فَبِهُداهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ** — *They are the ones whom Allah has guided. So follow their guidance.* (6:90). So understand this.

His saying: **آللهُ خَيْرٌ أَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** (Is Allah better, or the partners they ascribe [to Him]?) is part of the completion of the address to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the interrogation is for the purpose of affirmation (التقرير). The sum of the intended meaning is that if all praise is for Allah and He is the one who chooses His elect servants, then He is better than their deities whom they worship, who possess neither creation nor governance for which they could be praised, nor do they hold any good in their hands to bestow upon their worshippers.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمَّنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَأَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً** — *Is He who created the heavens and the earth, and sends down for you water from the sky...* (27:60) to the end of the verse. The word "gardens" (الحدائق) is the plural of *hadiqah* (حديقة), which is a garden that is bounded and enclosed by walls. "Of delightful beauty" (ذات بهجة) is an attribute of the gardens. It is stated in *Majma‘ al-Bayan*: "Of delightful beauty (ذات بهجة) means having a beautiful appearance that delights whoever sees it. He did not say *dhawat* (ذوات) [plural] because He intended the femininity of the collective group (الجماعة); had He intended the femininity of the individual entities (الأعيان), He would have said *dhawat*." End quote.

The word "or" (أَمْ) in the verse is "disjunctive" (منقطعة), providing the sense of "rather" (الإضراب). The word "He who" (مَنْ) is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is omitted, as is the other side of the alternative. The interrogation is for affirmation and to compel them to acknowledge the truth. The estimation (التقدير), based on what the context indicates, is: "Rather, is He who created the heavens and the earth... better, or that which they associate?" The matter follows this pattern in the four subsequent verses.

The meaning of the verse is: Rather, is He who created the heavens and the earth and sent down for you—meaning for your benefit—from the sky (السماء), which is the direction of height, water (ماء), which is rain, by which We caused to grow gardens of delightful beauty and radiance, the trees of which you were not—meaning you do not possess the power and it is not within your capacity—to cause to grow. Is there another god (إله) with Allah, the Glorified? This is a rhetorical question of denial (إنكار) and rebuke (توبيخ).

In the verse, there is a shift (التفات) from the third person (الغيبة) to the second person (الخطاب) regarding the polytheists. The subtle point (النكتة) in this is to intensify the rebuke by changing absence into presence. The station of the verses preceding this one was the station of a speaker addressing one of his close associates in the presence of his rebellious servants who have turned away from his servitude, venting his complaint to him while they listen. Once the proof (الحجة) was completed and the evidence (البينة) established—as in His saying: **آللهُ خَيْرٌ أَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** (Is Allah better, or the partners they ascribe?)—grief and regret were stirred. He then turned toward them after having turned away, beginning to compel them to acknowledge the truth by mentioning sign after sign, denying their polytheism and rebuking them for it, for their turning away from Him to others, for the lack of knowledge of most of them, and their lack of remembrance, despite His transcendence above their shirk and their lack of proof for what they claim.

His saying: **بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ يَعْدِلُونَ** — *Rather, they are a people who deviate.* (27:60). That is, they deviate from the truth to falsehood and from Allah, the Glorified, to others. It is also said: they equate others with Allah and treat them as equals.

In this sentence, there is a shift from the second person to the third person regarding the polytheists, returning to the address of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The "rather" (الإضراب) here is to explain that there is no utility in continuing to try to bring them to the truth, for they are deviators from it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمَّنْ جَعَلَ الْأَرْضَ قَراراً** — *Is He who made the earth an abode...* (27:61) to the end of the verse. "Abode" (القرار) is an infinitive used in the sense of the active participle, meaning "stable" or "settled." "Fissures" (الخلال) is the plural of *khalal*, which is the gap between two things. "Firm mountains" (الرواسي) is the plural of *rasiyah*, meaning "fixed," referring to the stable mountains. "Barrier" (الحاجز) is the preventer that intervenes between two things.

The meaning is: Rather, is He who made the earth stable so that it does not sway with you, and made rivers in the gaps within its interior, and made firm mountains for it, and placed between the two seas a barrier preventing them from mixing and blending—is He better, or that which they associate? The discussion regarding His saying: **أَإِلهٌ مَعَ اللهِ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** (Is there a god besides Allah? Rather, most of them do not know) is the same as the discussion regarding its counterpart in the previous verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمَّنْ يُجِيبُ الْمُضْطَرَّ إِذا دَعاهُ وَيَكْشِفُ السُّوءَ وَيَجْعَلُكُمْ خُلَفاءَ الْأَرْضِ أَإِلهٌ مَعَ اللهِ قَلِيلاً ما تَذَكَّرُونَ** — *Is He who answers the distressed one when he calls Him, and removes the distress, and makes you successors on the earth? Is there a god besides Allah? Little is the heed you take.* (27:62). What is meant by answering the distressed one when he calls Him is the responding to the supplication of the supplicants and the fulfilling of their needs. The description of "distress" (الاضطرار) was taken so that the reality of supplication (الدعاء) and petition (المسألة) is realized from the supplicant. For as long as a human being has not fallen into the straits of distress and remains in a state of sufficiency regarding the objective, the request does not become pure and sincere; this is evident.

Then He restricted it with His saying: **إِذا دَعاهُ** (when he calls Him) to indicate that the one called must be Allah, the Glorified. This only occurs when the supplicant is cut off from all apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) and his heart becomes attached to his Lord alone. As for the one whose heart is attached to apparent causes only, or to a combination of his Lord and those causes, he is not truly calling upon his Lord; he is calling upon another.

So, if the supplication is sincere and the one called is his Lord alone, then He, the Exalted, answers him and removes the evil (السوء) that compelled him to the petition, as He, the Exalted, said: **ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ** — *Call Me, I will answer you.* (40:60). He did not stipulate for the response anything except that there be a true supplication and that this supplication be directed to Him alone. He also said: **وَإِذا سَأَلَكَ عِبادِي عَنِّي فَإِنِّي قَرِيبٌ أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ** — *When My servants ask you about Me, [tell them that] I am indeed nearmost. I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me.* (2:186). We have detailed the meaning of supplication (الدعاء) in the second volume of this book under that verse.

From the preceding explanation, the invalidity of the claim made by some becomes clear—that the definite article (ال) in "the distressed" (الْمُضْطَرَّ) is for the genus (الجنس) rather than for exhaustion/universality (الاستغراق), arguing that many distressed people call out but are not answered, so the meaning is the answering of the distressed one's call in some cases, not all.

The reason for its invalidity is that verses such as: **ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ** (Call Me, I will answer you) and His saying: **فَإِنِّي قَرِيبٌ أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ** (I am indeed nearmost. I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me) refuse the failure of the response to follow the supplication. As for the claim that "many a distressed person calls but is not answered," it is not accepted if it is a true supplication to Allah, the Glorified, alone, as previously explained.

Furthermore, there are many verses indicating that a human being, upon distress—such as when riding a ship—turns toward his Lord and calls Him with sincerity (الإخلاص), and is answered, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذا مَسَّ الْإِنْسانَ الضُّرُّ دَعانا لِجَنْبِهِ أَوْ قاعِداً أَوْ قائِماً** — *When distress befalls man, he supplicates Us, [lying] on his side, or sitting, or standing...* (10:12), and His saying: **حَتَّى إِذا كُنْتُمْ فِي الْفُلْكِ** (until, when you are in the ships...) until His saying: **وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ أُحِيطَ بِهِمْ دَعَوُا اللهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ** — *...and they think they are besieged, they invoke Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him...* (10:22). How can the soul be conceived as attaching itself through its instinctive primordial nature (الفطرة) to a matter in which it has no confidence? The judgment of the primordial nature in this regard is exactly like its judgment when perceiving its existential need for the one who brings it into existence and governs its affairs: that there is a reality that fulfills its need, and that is Allah, the Glorified.

If you say: We often seek recourse (نتوسل) for our needs through apparent causes whose actual effectiveness in removing our need we are not certain of, and we only attach ourselves to them in the hope that they might benefit us if they do.

I say: This is an intellectual recourse (توسل فكري) whose origin is greed and hope, and it is different from the instinctive primordial recourse (التوسل الغريزي الفطري). Yes, within it is a type of instinctive turning, which is the seeking of a cause through the absolute concept of "cause," and the absolute cause never fails. So understand.

The invalidity of the claim that "the distressed one when he calls Him" refers to the sinner when he seeks forgiveness—for Allah forgives him, and that is His answering—also becomes clear.

The problem with this is that the issue of universality (الاستغراق) remains as it is: not every seeking of forgiveness is followed by forgiveness, nor is every seeker of forgiveness forgiven. Furthermore, there is no evidence to restrict the absolute term "distressed" to the disobedient sinner.

Some have mentioned: The universality remains, but the response should be restricted by [Allah's] will (المشية), as occurred in His saying, the Exalted: **فَيَكْشِفُ ما تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ إِنْ شاءَ** — *...then He will remove that for which you supplicated Him, if He wishes...* (6:41).

The problem with this is that the verse [6:41] occurs in a context that makes it unsuitable for restricting the response in the verse of the distressed. That context is His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتاكُمْ عَذابُ اللهِ أَوْ أَتَتْكُمُ السَّاعَةُ أَغَيْرَ اللهِ تَدْعُونَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ بَلْ إِيَّاهُ تَدْعُونَ فَيَكْشِفُ ما تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ إِنْ شاءَ** — *Say, ‘Tell me, should Allah’s punishment come to you, or should the Hour come to you, will you supplicate anyone other than Allah, should you be truthful? Rather Him you will supplicate, and He will remove that for which you supplicated Him, if He wishes...* (6:40-41). The Hour (الساعة) is of the decreed destiny (القضاء المحتوم) for which no true request for removal can be attached. As for the divine punishment, if the request for its removal is through true repentance and faith, then Allah removes it, as He removed it from the people of Yunus. If it is not so, but rather a ruse to escape it, then it is not removed because it is not a true request, but rather a plot in the form of a request, as Allah narrated regarding Pharaoh when drowning overtook him: **قالَ آمَنْتُ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا الَّذِي آمَنَتْ بِهِ بَنُوا إِسْرائيلَ وَأَنَا مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ آلْآنَ وَقَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ وَكُنْتَ مِنَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ** — *...he said, ‘I believe that there is no god except Him in whom the Children of Israel believe, and I am one of those who submit [to Him].’ [He was told,] ‘What! Now? When you were disobedient before and were among the agents of corruption?’* (10:90-91). He also narrated regarding other peoples seized by punishment: **قالُوا يا وَيْلَنا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ فَما زالَتْ تِلْكَ دَعْواهُمْ حَتَّى جَعَلْناهُمْ حَصِيداً خامِدِينَ** — *They said, ‘Woe to us! We were indeed wrongdoers.’ That remained their cry until We made them like a mown field, extinct.* (21:14-15).

In short, the instance of His saying: **فَيَكْشِفُ ما تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ إِنْ شاءَ** (then He will remove that for which you supplicated Him, if He wishes), since it was something where the request could be true or not true, it was necessary to restrict the removal and the response therein by the will; so Allah removes it from them if He wishes—and that is in the instance of a true request and faith—and He does not remove it if He does not wish. This is different from the instance of the verse of the distressed and other verses of answering the call, which involve the reality of supplication to Allah, the Glorified, alone.

His saying: **وَيَجْعَلُكُمْ خُلَفاءَ الْأَرْضِ** (and makes you successors on the earth). What the context provides is that the intended meaning of succession (الخلافة) is the earthly succession that Allah has granted to the human being, whereby he disposes of the earth and the creation within it as he wishes, as He, the Exalted, said: **وَإِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي جاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً** — *When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth...’* (2:30).

This is because his disposals (تصرفات) which he carries out in the earth and what is in it by virtue of his succession are matters linked to his life and related to his livelihood. The evil (السوء) that brings him to the point of distress and for which he asks Allah's removal is inevitably something that prevents him from disposal or some disposal therein, and closes upon him the door of life, survival, and what pertains to them, or some of those doors. Thus, in the removal of evil from him, his succession is completed.

This meaning becomes even clearer if the supplication and petition in His saying: **إِذا دَعاهُ** (when he calls Him) is taken to be more general than verbal supplication, as is apparent from His saying, the Exalted: **وَآتاكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها** — *He gave you all that you asked Him. If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them.* (14:34), and His saying: **يَسْئَلُهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — *Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him.* (55:29). For on this basis, all that the human being has been given and provided with in terms of disposals are instances of the removal of evil from the distressed and needy one following his supplication. Thus, making him a successor follows the answering of his supplication and the removal of the evil that distressed him.

It is said: The meaning is "and makes you successors of the nations before you on the earth," inhabiting their dwellings and disposing of them after them. This, however, is less suitable to the context than the meaning we have presented.

It is also said: The meaning is "and makes you successors of the disbelievers by inheriting their lands and obeying Allah after their polytheism and obstinacy." The problem with this is that the address in the verse, like the other five verses before it, is to the disbelievers, not the believers, as this view would require.

His saying: **قَلِيلاً ما تَذَكَّرُونَ** (Little is the heed you take) is a rebuking address to the disbelievers. It is also read *yadhakkarun* (يذكرون) with a *ya* for the third person, which is preferable as it matches what is at the end of the other five verses, such as His saying: **بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ يَعْدِلُونَ** (Rather, they are a people who deviate) and **بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** (Rather, most of them do not know), for the address in all of them is to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله by way of shift (التفات), as previously explained.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمَّنْ يَهْدِيكُمْ فِي ظُلُماتِ الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ وَمَنْ يُرْسِلُ الرِّياحَ بُشْراً بَيْنَ يَدَيْ رَحْمَتِهِ** — *Is He who guides you in the darkness of land and sea, and who sends the winds as heralds of His mercy?* (27:63) etc. What is meant by the darkness of land and sea is the darkness of the nights in the land and sea; this involves a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي). What is meant by sending the winds as heralds is sending them as bringers of glad tidings of rain shortly before its descent, and "mercy" (الرحمة) is the rain. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمَّنْ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ وَمَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ** — *Is He who originates the creation then restores it, and who provides for you from the sky and the earth?* (27:64) etc. Originating the creation (بدء الخلق) is bringing it into existence for the first time, and restoring it (إعادته) is returning it to Him through the Resurrection (البعث). The silencing (تبكيت) of the polytheists through the beginning and the restoration—despite their denial of the Resurrection, as He will mention in His saying: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا** (And the faithless say...) etc.—is based on the fact that the Return is established by definitive proofs in His speech. Thus, it was taken as an accepted premise, and then their denial or doubt regarding it was addressed in the following verses.

It is said: What is meant by originating the creation then restoring it is the bringing into existence of an individual of a species, then its destruction, and the bringing into existence of its like after it. In short, the bringing into existence of like after like. Thus, the objection that the polytheists deny the Return, so how can it be used as an argument against them, is avoided. This, however, is far from the apparent meaning of the verse.

What the verse contains of the subtle Quranic truths indicates that there is no absolute annihilation (بطلان) in existence; rather, what Allah, the Exalted, brought into existence through the beginning shall return to Him through the restoration. What we witness of destruction therein is merely our loss of it after having found it.

As for what the theologians (المتكلمون) have agreed upon regarding the impossibility of restoring the non-existent (إعادة المعدوم) in some existents like accidents (الأعراض), and their disagreement regarding the permissibility of restoring others like substances (الجواهر), it has no connection to the issue of the Resurrection as established by the verse. For the Resurrection is not of the nature of restoring the non-existent such that it would be impossible if its restoration were impossible; rather, the Resurrection is the return of the creation and its coming back—which is a creation without annihilation—to its Lord who originated it.

His saying: **وَمَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (and who provides for you from the sky and the earth) is an allusion to what occurs of His governance of their affairs between the beginning and the return, which is their provision through celestial causes like rains and their factors, and terrestrial causes like all that the human being feeds upon from the earth.

His saying: **قُلْ هاتُوا بُرْهانَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** — *Say, ‘Produce your proof, if you are truthful.’* (27:64). When He, the Glorified, mentioned sections encompassing the whole of creation and governance, while alluding to the connection of governance to one another and the connection of all to creation—whereby creation and governance became a single matter attributed to Him and subsisting through Him, the Exalted—He thereby established that He, the Exalted, is the Lord of everything alone, without partner. The necessary consequence of this was the invalidation of the divinity (ألوهية) of the deities they call upon besides Allah.

This is because divinity—which is the entitlement to worship—follows Lordship (الربوبية), which is governance (تدبير) arising from ownership (ملك). Worship, as they practice it, is either to be gratitude for a blessing or protection from a calamity; in either case, it is linked to governance, which is among the affairs of Lordship.

Since the invalidation of the divinity of deities besides Allah was the purpose of the sections presented in these verses—as indicated by His saying after presenting each section: **أَإِلهٌ مَعَ اللهِ** (Is there a god besides Allah?)—He commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with His saying: **قُلْ هاتُوا بُرْهانَكُمْ** (Say, ‘Produce your proof’) to demand from them the proof for what they claim regarding the divinity of their deities, so that it may become clear through their inability that they are making arbitrary claims. For if they were to argue for their divinity with anything, they would have to attribute to them some part of the governance of the world, whereas all creation and governance belong to Him, the Exalted, alone.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ لا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ الْغَيْبَ إِلَّا اللهُ وَما يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ** — *Say, ‘No one in the heavens and the earth knows the Unseen except Allah, and they do not know when they will be resurrected.’* (27:65). When He commanded him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, after invalidating the divinity of their deities by attributing creation and governance to Him, the Exalted, alone, to demand proof from them for what they claim, He commanded him a second time to confront them with another proof for the invalidation of the divinity of their deities: their lack of knowledge of the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — and their lack of awareness of the Hour and when they will be resurrected. This is while no one among those in the heavens and the earth—including their deities who are angels, jinn, and saintly humans—knows the Unseen, nor do they know when they will be resurrected. Had they been gods possessing the governance of creation—and among governance is the reward on the Day of Resurrection—they would have known the Hour.

It has become clear through this explanation that His saying: **لا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ الْغَيْبَ إِلَّا اللهُ** (No one in the heavens and the earth knows the Unseen except Allah) is an independent proof for the invalidation of the divinity of their deities and the exclusivity of divinity to Him, the Exalted, alone. His saying: **وَما يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ** (and they do not know when they will be resurrected) is the conjoining of the clearest and most important instances of the Unseen to it in terms of knowledge regarding the matter of governance.

It has also become clear that the two plural pronouns in **وَما يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ** (and they do not know when they will be resurrected) refer to "those in the heavens and the earth," for the explanation would not be complete without it.

As for the claim of some that the pronoun refers to the polytheists—even if the lack of awareness of what was mentioned is general—to avoid a disconnect (تفكيك) between it and the subsequent pronouns which certainly refer to them, it is problematic. This is because it contradicts the purpose for which the noble verse was brought, as we have previously indicated. A disconnect between pronouns when a contextual clue (قرينة) exists is not problematic.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **بَلِ ادَّارَكَ عِلْمُهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِنْها بَلْ هُمْ مِنْها عَمُونَ** — *Do they have a full knowledge of the Hereafter? Rather, they are in doubt about it. Rather, they are blind to it.* (27:66). The word **ادارك** (*iddaraka*) is originally *tadāraka*. *Tadāruk* is the following of parts of a thing one after another until they are exhausted and nothing remains of them. The meaning of "their knowledge has been exhausted (تدارك) regarding the Hereafter" is that they spent what knowledge they had on other things until their knowledge was depleted, and nothing remained of it by which they could perceive the matter of the Hereafter, similar to His saying, the Exalted: **فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْ مَنْ تَوَلَّى عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحياةَ الدُّنْيا ذلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ** — *So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge.* (53:29-30). "Blind" (عَمُونَ) is the plural of *‘amin*.

When His, the Exalted, argumentation reached the mention of the lack of awareness of anyone other than Him, the Exalted, regarding the time of the Resurrection and the silencing of the polytheists thereby, He returned to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and reminded him that they are excluded from being addressed by that. For they have no news of anything regarding the matters of the Hereafter, let alone the time of the standing of the Hour. This is because they spent what knowledge they had on the aspects of the life of this world; thus, they are in absolute ignorance regarding the matters of the Hereafter. Rather, they are in doubt (شك) about the Hereafter, wavering in its matter, as appears from their arguments for its negation based on improbability. Rather, they are blind (عمون) to it; Allah has blinded their hearts from testifying to it and believing in its existence.

It has become clear through this explanation that the repetition of the word of "rather" (الإضراب) is to explain the levels of deprivation from knowledge of the Hereafter, and that they are at the highest of them. His saying: **بَلِ ادَّارَكَ عِلْمُهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ** (Do they have a full knowledge of the Hereafter?) means they have no knowledge of it, as if it has never struck their ears. His saying: **بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِنْها** (Rather, they are in doubt about it) means its news has struck their ears and entered their hearts, but they wavered and did not believe in it. His saying: **بَلْ هُمْ مِنْها عَمُونَ** (Rather, they are blind to it) means they did not merely cease to believe in it of their own accord and by their own choice; rather, Allah, the Glorified, blinded the eyes of their hearts, so they became blind. Thus, it is far-fetched that they should perceive anything of its matter.

It is said: What is meant by the "attainment" (تدارك) of their knowledge is its perfection and its reaching the level of certainty due to the perfection of the proofs indicating the truth of the Resurrection, and the sentence is intended as sarcasm (تهكم). The problem with this is that it does not suit the subsequent "rather" involving doubt and blindness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَإِذا كُنَّا تُراباً وَآباؤُنا أَإِنَّا لَمُخْرَجُونَ** — *The faithless say, ‘What! When we and our fathers have become dust, shall we really be brought forth [from the grave]?* (27:67) to His saying: **الْأَوَّلِينَ** (of the ancients). This is a narration of an argument from them to negate the Resurrection based on improbability. That is: how is it possible that we should be brought forth from the earth as complete human beings as we are today, when we have died and become dust, both we and our fathers likewise?

His saying: **لَقَدْ وُعِدْنا هذا نَحْنُ وَآباؤُنا مِنْ قَبْلُ** — *We and our fathers were certainly promised this before...* (27:68) is another argument from them based on improbability. That is: we were certainly promised this—meaning the Resurrection after death—we and our fathers, before this Prophet promised us this. Those who promised before were the past prophets. So it is an ancient promise that we have continued to be promised; had it been a true report and a real promise, it would have occurred by this day. Since it has not occurred, it is among the myths (خرافات) fabricated by the ancients, who were fond of fabricating delusions and myths and listening to them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ** — *Say, ‘Travel over the land and observe how was the fate of the guilty.’* (27:69). This is a warning and a frightening of them for their denial of the prophets' promise of the Resurrection, by commanding them to travel through the land and observe what was the end of the criminals (المجرمين) who denied the prophets who warned them of the Resurrection. For in observing the end of their affair, as indicated by their ruined dwellings and empty lands, there is sufficiency for those who take heed among the possessors of insight. In expressing the deniers as "criminals," there is a kindness toward the believers in their avoidance of crimes. So it is said.

It is possible to establish the verse as a proof indicating the Return. The approximation of this is that the end of the criminals' affair in the punishment of extirpation is proof that crime and injustice are of a nature that warrants being taken to task, and that an action—whether it be an act of excellence or a crime—is preserved for its doer and he will be held accountable for it. Since the entirety of this accounting and reward—especially for righteous deeds (الصالحات)—does not occur in this world, it must inevitably be in another world, which is the Abode of the Hereafter.

Thus, the verse would be in the sense of His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ** — *Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?* (38:28). This interpretation is supported by His saying: **عاقِبَةُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ** (the fate of the guilty). Had the intention been to threaten the deniers of the messengers and frighten them, it would have been more suitable to say: "the fate of the deniers," as previously indicated.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا تَكُنْ فِي ضَيْقٍ مِمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ** — *Do not grieve for them, and do not be upset by their plotting.* (27:70). That is: let not their persistence in disbelief and denial grieve you, and let not your breast be straitened by their plotting to invalidate your call and their barring people from the way of Allah. For they are under the eye of Allah and they cannot frustrate Him, and He will soon requite them for their deeds.

The verse is intended to comfort the soul of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. His saying: **وَلا تَكُنْ فِي ضَيْقٍ** (and do not be upset) etc., is conjoined to what precedes it as an explanatory conjunction.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ** — *They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’* (27:71). It appears that what is meant by the promise is the promise of the punishment of requital, more general than this world and the Hereafter. The context supports this, and the rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ عَسى أَنْ يَكُونَ رَدِفَ لَكُمْ بَعْضُ الَّذِي تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ** — *Say, ‘It may be that some of what you seek to hasten has already come close behind you.’* (27:72). They said: The *lam* in **رَدِفَ لَكُمْ** (*radifa lakum*) is extra for emphasis, like the *ba* in His saying: **وَلا تُلْقُوا بِأَيْدِيكُمْ إِلَى التَّهْلُكَةِ** — *...and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction...* (2:195). The meaning is: it has followed you and caught up with you. It is also said that *radifa* implies the meaning of a verb that takes its object with a *lam*.

What is meant by "some of what they seek to hasten" is the punishment of this world before the punishment of the Hereafter. For they were seeking to hasten the fulfillment of what Allah promised them of the decisive judgment, which is inseparable from their punishment. Their punishment in this world is some of the punishment they seek to hasten by demanding the fulfillment of the promise. Perhaps the verse refers to the punishment of the Day of Badr, as has been said.

They said: "Perhaps" (*‘asa*) and "maybe" (*la‘alla*) from Allah, the Exalted, are mandatory (واجب), because the reality of hope (الترجي) is based on ignorance, and that is not permissible for Him, the Exalted. So the meaning of His saying: **عَسى أَنْ يَكُونَ رَدِفَ لَكُمْ** is: the punishment will surely follow you and come to you as a certainty.

The problem with this is that the meaning of hope, wishing, and the like, just as it is permissible to subsist in the soul of the speaker, it is permissible to subsist in the context, or in the listener, or others. In His speech, the Exalted, it subsists in someone other than the speaker, such as the context and so on. Since the answer in the verse is returned to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, the hope indicated by the word *‘asa* subsists in his noble soul. The meaning is: Say, I hope that the punishment has followed close behind you.

In the *Tafsir* of Abu al-Su‘ud: "*‘Asa*, *la‘alla*, and *sawfa* in the promises of kings are equivalent to certainty. They only use them to display dignity and to signal that a hint from the likes of them is like an explicit statement from others. The promise and threat of Allah, the Exalted, follow this course." End quote. This is a sound view.

The meaning of the verse is: Say to these questioners about the time of the promise: I hope that some of the promise you seek to hasten has followed you, which is the punishment of this world that brings you near to the punishment of the Hereafter and leads you to it. In the expression **رَدِفَ لَكُمْ** (has come close behind you), there is an allusion to its proximity.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَشْكُرُونَ** — *Indeed your Lord is gracious to mankind, but most of them do not give thanks.* (27:73). The meaning of the verse in itself is clear. Its occurrence in the context of threat and frightening indicates that His, the Exalted, delaying the punishment from them despite their deserving it is only a grace (فضل) from Him upon them, for which they must thank Him, but they do not thank Him and instead ask for its hastening.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَيَعْلَمُ ما تُكِنُّ صُدُورُهُمْ وَما يُعْلِنُونَ** — *Indeed your Lord knows what their breasts conceal and what they disclose.* (27:74). That is: the delay of the punishment is not due to ignorance on His part, the Exalted, regarding their state and what they deserve of disbelief and denial; for He knows what their breasts hide and conceal, and what they manifest.

Then He emphasized this by stating that every hidden thing (غائبة)—which is that which is of a nature to be absent and hidden in any part of the world—is written and preserved with Him, the Exalted. This is His saying: **وَما مِنْ غائِبَةٍ فِي السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ** — *There is no invisible thing in the heaven and the earth but it is in a manifest Book.* (27:75).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَقُصُّ عَلى بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ** — *Indeed this Quran relates to the Children of Israel...* (27:76) to His saying: **الْعَزِيزُ الْعَلِيمُ** — *...the All-mighty, the All-knowing.* (27:78). This is a comfort for the soul of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, a preparation for what He will mention regarding the truth of his call, and a strengthening of the faith of the believers in him. In this way, it connects to His saying earlier: **وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ** (Do not grieve for them) etc., which intimates the truth of his call.

His saying: **إِنَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَقُصُّ عَلى بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ أَكْثَرَ الَّذِي هُمْ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ** — *Indeed this Quran relates to the Children of Israel most of what they differ about.* (27:76) refers to what the Quran relates of the stories of the prophets and explains the truth in what they differed about regarding their affairs—among which is the matter of the Messiah (peace be upon him)—and explains the truth in what they differed about regarding cognitions and laws.

His saying: **وَإِنَّهُ لَهُدىً وَرَحمةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — *and it is indeed a guidance and mercy for the faithful.* (27:77) indicates that it guides the believers through what it relates to the Children of Israel toward the truth, and that it is a mercy for them by which their hearts find peace and faith is established in their souls thereby.

His saying: **إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ بِحُكْمِهِ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْعَلِيمُ** — *Indeed your Lord will judge between them by His judgment, and He is the All-mighty, the All-knowing.* (27:78) is an allusion to the fact that the judgment between them belongs to Allah; He is your Lord, the All-mighty (العزيز) who is not overcome in His affair, the All-knowing (العليم) who is not ignorant and does not err in His judgment. He is the Judge between them by His judgment. So let the soul of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله be content with his Lord, the All-mighty, the All-knowing, as a Judge and Arbiter, and let him return the matter to Him, as he should do likewise regarding the polytheists: "do not grieve for them, and do not be upset by their plotting."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ إِنَّكَ عَلَى الْحَقِّ الْمُبِينِ** — *So put your trust in Allah; indeed you stand on the manifest truth.* (27:79). This is a derivation from the sum of what he was commanded to do in the face of the disbelievers' disbelief and the Children of Israel's differences. That is: the affair of all of them belongs to Allah, not to you; so take Him as a trustee (وكيلاً), for He is sufficient for you, and do not fear anything, for you are in the security of the truth.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّكَ لا تُسْمِعُ الْمَوْتى** — *You cannot make the dead hear...* (27:80) to His saying: **فَهُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ** — *...so they submit.* (27:81). This is a justification for the command to trust [in Allah]. That is: We only commanded you to trust in Allah regarding the matter of their faith and disbelief because they are dead, and it is not within your power to make the dead hear your call. They are deaf and do not hear, and blind and lost; you are not able to make the deaf hear when they turn their backs—perhaps He restricted the inability to make the deaf hear with His saying: **إِذا وَلَّوْا مُدْبِرِينَ** (when they turn their backs) because if they were not turning away, it might be possible to make them understand through some kind of sign—nor can you guide the blind out of their error. Rather, the one you are able to make hear is he who believes in Our signs (آياتنا) indicating Us, and you guide them, for due to their submission to those true proofs, they are submissive (مسلمون) to Us, testifying to what they indicate.

Through this explanation, it has become clear:
First: That what is meant by "making hear" (الإسماع) is guidance (الهداية).
Second: That what is meant by "signs" (الآيات) are the proofs indicating monotheism and the true cognitions that follow it.
Third: That whoever perceives the true proofs from the signs of the horizons and the souls with a sound intellect, then submits to them through faith and compliance, is not among the dead, nor among those whose hearing and sight Allah has sealed.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَسَلامٌ عَلى عِبادِهِ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفى** (and peace be to His servants whom He has chosen), he said: "They are the Progeny of Muhammad (عليه‌السلام)."

**I say:** It was also narrated in *Jam‘ al-Jawami‘* from them (peace be upon them) as a disconnected (مرسلاً) and unnamed (مضمراً) report. You have already learned from the preceding explanation under the verse that what the context provides is that the intended meaning—according to the instance of the verse—is the prophets who were blessed with the favor of election, and Allah has narrated the stories of a group of them. Thus, his saying (peace be upon him)—if the narration is authentic—"They are the Progeny of Muhammad (عليه‌السلام)" is of the nature of "flow and application" (الجري والانطباق) — *the principle that Quranic verses apply to new instances beyond their original context*.

Similar to it is what was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from several authors of books, from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding the verse: "They are the companions of Muhammad." This—if the narration is authentic—is an instance of "flow and application" on his part.

From this, the nature of what was also narrated from ‘Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn Jarir from Sufyan al-Thawri regarding the verse becomes clear: "It was revealed specifically concerning the companions of Muhammad." There is neither a specific revelation (نزول) nor exclusivity (اختصاص).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, also regarding His saying, the Exalted: **بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ يَعْدِلُونَ** (Rather, they are a people who deviate), he said: "From the truth."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَمَّنْ يُجِيبُ الْمُضْطَرَّ إِذا دَعاهُ** (Is He who answers the distressed one when he calls Him), it is narrated: My father narrated to me from al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali ibn Faddal from Salih ibn ‘Uqbah from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "It was revealed concerning the Riser (القائم) from the Progeny of Muhammad (عليه‌السلام). He, by Allah, is the distressed one when he prays two units of prayer at the Station [of Ibrahim] and calls upon Allah (عز وجل); so He answers him, removes the evil, and makes him a successor on the earth."

**I say:** The narration is also of the nature of "flow," while the verse is general.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Tabarani narrated from Sa‘d ibn Junadah, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Whoever separates from the community (الجماعة) shall be in the Fire on his face—because Allah, the Exalted, says: **أَمَّنْ يُجِيبُ الْمُضْطَرَّ إِذا دَعاهُ ـ وَيَكْشِفُ السُّوءَ وَيَجْعَلُكُمْ خُلَفاءَ الْأَرْضِ** (Is He who answers the distressed one when he calls Him, and removes the distress, and makes you successors on the earth?). For succession (الخلافة) is from Allah (عز وجل); if it is good, He lets it proceed, and if it is evil, he is taken to task for it. You must adhere to obedience in what Allah has commanded."

**I say:** The narration is not without issues. It has already been mentioned that the intended meaning of succession in the verse—as witnessed by the context—is the earthly succession decreed for every human being, which is the authority over what is on the earth through various types of disposal, not succession in the sense of government over the nation by managing the wheel of their society.

Even disregarding that, the text of the narration is not free from internal contradiction. If the meaning of succession being "from Allah" is that his authority over the people is by a decree from Allah—in other words, its ontological attribution (الانتساب التكويني) to Allah, the Glorified, as mentioned regarding the kingdom of Nimrod in His saying: **أَنْ آتاهُ اللهُ الْمُلْكَ** — *...because Allah had given him the kingdom...* (2:258), and His saying narrating from Pharaoh: **أَلَيْسَ لِي مُلْكُ مِصْرَ** — *...‘Does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me...’* (43:51)—then it is clear that succession in this sense does not necessitate the obligation of obedience and the prohibition of opposition. Otherwise, it would be a violation of the foundation of the religious call and an obligation to obey the likes of Nimrod and Pharaoh, of which there are many examples. If the meaning is the religious conventional designation (الجعل الوضعي الديني)—in other words, its legislative attribution (الانتساب التشريعي) to Allah, the Exalted—and then obedience to him became obligatory in what he commands even if it were a sin, that would be an explicit violation of the laws. If the obligation is to obey him in other than sin against Allah—due to his saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "There is no obedience to a creature in disobedience to the Creator"—then separating from the community would be permissible in some cases, which contradicts the beginning of the narration.

A similar problem applies to his saying at the end: "You must adhere to obedience in what Allah has commanded." If what is meant by "what Allah has commanded" is the obedience to the position of succession even if it involves sin, it would be an explicit violation of the legislation of laws. If what is meant is the obedience to Allah even if it necessitates disobedience to the position of succession, it would contradict the beginning of the narration.

It has become clear today through social research that the endorsement of a government by one who does not respect the prevailing sacred laws is something no rational and mature society would accept. Thus, it is mandatory to transcend the station of the Legislator of the religion from such a thing. To say that the interest of preserving the unity of the word and the agreement of the nation is more important than preserving some laws through separation means the permissibility of destroying the reality of the religion to preserve its name.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, also narrated by al-Tayalisi, Sa‘id ibn Mansur, Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Asma’ wa al-Sifat*, from Masruq, who said: I was reclining with ‘A’ishah when she said: "There are three things; whoever speaks of one of them has fabricated a great lie against Allah." I asked: "What are they?" She said: "Whoever claims that Muhammad saw his Lord has fabricated a great lie against Allah." He said: I was reclining, so I sat up and said: "O Mother of the Believers, bear with me and do not be hasty with me. Did Allah not say: **وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ بِالْأُفُقِ الْمُبِينِ** (He certainly saw him on the manifest horizon) and **وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرى** (He certainly saw him yet another time)?"

She said: "I am the first of this nation to ask the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about this. He said: 'It was Gabriel. I did not see him in the form in which he was created except these two times. I saw him descending from the sky, filling the greatness of his creation what is between the sky and the earth.'" She said: "Did you not hear Allah (عز وجل) say: **لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصارُ وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصارَ ـ وَهُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ** (The sights do not perceive Him, but He perceives the sights, and He is the All-attentive, the All-aware)? Or did you not hear Allah say: **وَما كانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُكَلِّمَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا وَحْياً** (It is not [possible] for any human that Allah should speak to him except through revelation...) until His saying: **عَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ** (All-exalted, All-wise)?"

"And whoever claims that Muhammad concealed anything from the Book of Allah has fabricated a great lie against Allah, while Allah, majestic is His mention, says: **يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ ـ وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَما بَلَّغْتَ رِسالَتَهُ وَاللهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ** (O Messenger! Proclaim what has been sent down to you from your Lord; and if you do not, you will not have proclaimed His message, and Allah shall protect you from people)."

She said: "And whoever claims that he informs people of what will be tomorrow has fabricated a great lie against Allah, while Allah, the Exalted, says: **قُلْ لا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ الْغَيْبَ إِلَّا اللهُ** (Say, ‘No one in the heavens and the earth knows the Unseen except Allah’)."

**I say:** There is something in the text of the narration. As for the verses of vision (الرؤية), they only negate sensory vision, not the vision of the heart (رؤية القلب), which is a type of vision beyond faith (الإيمان) that is mere conviction (الاعتقاد); we have discussed this extensively in the appropriate places.

As for His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ** (O Messenger! Proclaim...), we have clarified in the commentary of that verse that it is specific, not general. Even if it were assumed to be general, it only indicates that everything sent down to him containing a message (رسالة) must be proclaimed. It is possible that something was sent down to him whose knowledge was exclusive to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, so he concealed it from others.

As for His saying: **قُلْ لا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ الْغَيْبَ إِلَّا اللهُ** (Say, ‘No one in the heavens and the earth knows the Unseen except Allah’), it only indicates the essential exclusivity (الاختصاص بالذات) of the knowledge of the Unseen to Him, the Exalted, like other verses of the exclusivity of the Unseen to Him. It does not negate another's knowledge of it through teaching from Him, the Exalted, as indicated by His saying: **عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ** — *Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves...* (72:26-27). Allah, the Glorified, narrated a similar type of informing from the Messiah (peace be upon him) when he said: **وَأُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما تَأْكُلُونَ وَما تَدَّخِرُونَ** — *...and I will inform you of what you eat and what you store up...* (3:49). It is well known that the one who says the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to inform people of what would be tomorrow does not negate that this was through teaching from Allah to him.

The reports have reached the level of frequency (تواترت)—despite their dispersion and variety—from the paths of both sects regarding his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله informing of many future events.
